Category: Transportation & Logistics: Aviation, Maritime, Logistics, Shipping, Automotive
Description: Fake instructions or impersonation of control authorities.
Prerequisites: Learners should have a basic familiarity with transport or logistics operations, such as working in aviation, maritime, shipping, or road freight environments, and a general comfort with using digital communication tools in a professional setting.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key characteristics of deepfake audio and video content used to impersonate control authorities, and explain how fake instructions can pose serious safety and security risks across aviation, maritime, and logistics operations. You will also apply practical verification techniques to recognise suspicious communications and respond appropriately when the authenticity of operational instructions is in doubt.

lessons.Description: This lesson introduces transport operations staff to the growing threat of deepfake technology, focusing on how criminals can use realistic fake audio, video, or text to impersonate control authorities and issue fraudulent instructions. You will learn to recognise the warning signs of manipulated communications and understand why verifying the source of any unusual or high risk instruction is essential. The lesson covers practical steps for confirming the identity of those giving operational directives, particularly in time-pressured situations where staff may be more vulnerable to deception. By the end of this lesson, you will have a clearer understanding of how deepfake risks apply directly to your working environment and what actions you can take to protect yourself and your organisation.
lessons.content: Fake instructions or impersonation of control authorities.

slides.subtitle: Understanding AI-generated fake communications
slides.slidecontent:
• Deepfakes use artificial intelligence to create realistic fake audio, video, and text
• Advanced enough to fool experienced transport professionals
• In our industry, fake instructions can redirect vehicles, close routes, or trigger emergencies
• Creating convincing impersonations of authority figures is now technically straightforward
• This threat is rapidly expanding across all transport sectors

slides.subtitle: Characteristics of deceptive communications
slides.slidecontent:
• Mimic authentic tone, language, and protocols of real control centres
• Often delivered with artificial urgency to bypass normal verification
• Voice deepfakes can replicate familiar controllers' speech patterns
• May arrive through unexpected channels like personal phones or messaging apps
• Designed to pressure immediate action without following standard procedures

slides.subtitle: Confirming authenticity before acting
slides.slidecontent:
• Never act on instructions without independent verification
• Use callback procedures through official contact directories
• Cross-check commands via second, separate communication channel
• Maintain updated lists of authorised personnel for your operations
• Trust established processes over individual voices, regardless of familiarity

slides.subtitle: Identifying suspicious instruction patterns
slides.slidecontent:
• Unexpected urgency demanding immediate action without checks
• Instructions arriving through unofficial or personal communication channels
• Requests to bypass normal authorisation or sign-off procedures
• Voice or video quality that seems unnatural or slightly 'off'
• Commands that leave no traceable record in official systems

slides.subtitle: Spotting deepfake audio and video clues
slides.slidecontent:
• Audio may contain subtle glitches, unnatural pauses, or inconsistent background noise
• Video deepfakes can show odd lip synchronisation or facial movements
• Visual texture may appear artificial or overly smooth in certain lighting
• Voice patterns might lack natural emotional variation
• Digital artifacts may be visible during rapid movements or gestures

slides.subtitle: Your action plan when receiving instructions
slides.slidecontent:
• Step 1: Pause and assess the instruction for unusual elements
• Step 2: Hang up and call back using verified official contact numbers
• Step 3: Confirm identity through secondary authentication methods
• Step 4: Cross-reference the instruction with official logs or systems
• Step 5: Document the original communication before proceeding

slides.subtitle: Taking action on suspicious communications
slides.slidecontent:
• Immediately inform your supervisor of any questionable instructions
• Document all details: content, delivery method, timestamp, and sender information
• Never compromise safety by acting on unverified orders to avoid delays
• Follow your organisation's formal reporting procedure for security incidents
• Remember that raising concerns protects everyone and is always appropriate

slides.subtitle: Daily practices for transport security
slides.slidecontent:
• Establish verification as your default response to all operational instructions
• Maintain current knowledge of authorised contacts and communication protocols
• Stay informed about emerging deepfake threats in your transport sector
• Practice recognition skills through regular security awareness updates
• Foster a workplace culture where questioning instructions is valued and encouraged

scripts.script: Welcome to this course on deepfake risks in transport operations. A deepfake is artificial intelligence used to create fake audio, video, or text messages that appear completely authentic. These aren't obvious forgeries - they're sophisticated enough to deceive experienced transport professionals who handle communications daily. In our industry, this creates serious risks. A convincing fake instruction could redirect vehicles to wrong destinations, unnecessarily close critical routes, or trigger costly emergency responses. What makes this particularly concerning is that the technology to impersonate control authorities is no longer complex or expensive. The tools are accessible, and their misuse is increasing across all transport sectors. Throughout this course, we'll examine how these threats manifest in your working environment and the practical steps you can take to protect your operations.

Understanding how fake authority instructions present themselves is crucial for detection. Deepfakes are specifically designed to replicate the exact tone, technical vocabulary, and communication style you expect from legitimate control centres. They can even reproduce the voice characteristics of controllers you know personally. However, these fake instructions typically share common patterns. They almost always create artificial urgency, pushing you to act quickly without following standard verification procedures. This pressure is deliberate - it's designed to bypass your natural caution. You should also be suspicious of instructions arriving through non-standard channels. If a message claiming to be from control arrives on your personal phone, through a messaging app, or via any unofficial communication method, treat it as highly suspect. Genuine authorities stick to established communication protocols and don't randomly change how they contact field personnel.

Effective verification is your primary defence against fake instructions. The fundamental principle is simple: never act on any operational command until you've independently confirmed its authenticity. This means using callback procedures - hang up and call the authority back using numbers from your official contact directory, not numbers provided by the caller. Always cross-check instructions through a separate communication channel from the one you received the original message on. Your organisation maintains authorised contact lists specifically for this purpose, so ensure you know where to find these and keep them current. Most importantly, learn to trust your established verification processes more than any individual voice, regardless of how familiar or authoritative it sounds. A genuine instruction will always withstand proper verification - if it doesn't, that tells you everything you need to know.

Recognising red flag warning signs can prevent you from falling victim to fake instructions. The most common indicator is artificial urgency - if someone is demanding immediate action without time for normal checks, treat this pressure itself as suspicious. Legitimate authority figures understand and respect proper verification procedures. Pay attention to how instructions arrive. Commands coming through personal phones, messaging apps, or other unofficial channels should immediately raise your suspicion. Be wary of any request asking you to skip normal authorisation steps, bypass sign-off procedures, or ignore standard safety protocols - this is exactly what attackers want you to do. Finally, if an instruction creates no traceable record in your official systems, leaves no documentation trail, or cannot be found in operational logs, consider this a serious warning sign that requires immediate verification.

While deepfake technology is increasingly sophisticated, technical imperfections can still reveal fake audio and video content. In audio deepfakes, listen for subtle inconsistencies like unnatural pauses, unusual background noise patterns, or slight voice quality variations. The speech may lack natural emotional inflection or sound overly consistent in tone. Video deepfakes often struggle with lip synchronisation, particularly during rapid speech, and facial movements may appear slightly artificial or disconnected from the audio. Look for visual artifacts around the edges of faces, unusual lighting on facial features, or skin textures that appear overly smooth or artificial. During video calls, watch for glitches during head movements or gestures, as these complex motions often expose technical limitations. While these detection methods aren't foolproof, they can provide valuable additional evidence when combined with other verification techniques.

When you receive any operational instruction, follow this systematic verification protocol. First, pause and evaluate the instruction for any unusual elements - urgency, unexpected channels, or requests to bypass procedures. Don't let pressure rush you past this initial assessment. Second, terminate the current communication and call back using official contact numbers from your verified directory. Never use callback numbers provided by the original caller. Third, confirm the identity of the person giving the instruction through secondary authentication - ask questions only the genuine authority would know. Fourth, cross-reference the instruction with your official operational systems, logs, or duty schedules to ensure consistency. Finally, document the original communication completely before proceeding. This protocol may seem lengthy, but it can be completed quickly and becomes automatic with practice.

When you encounter suspicious communications, swift and thorough reporting is essential. Immediately inform your direct supervisor about any instruction that seems questionable, regardless of how minor your concerns might seem. Document everything about the suspicious communication: the exact content, how it was delivered, the time you received it, any caller identification information, and what made you suspicious. This documentation could be crucial for investigating the incident and preventing future attempts. Never compromise safety by acting on unverified instructions just to avoid operational delays - a brief verification delay is insignificant compared to the potential consequences of following fake commands. Familiarise yourself with your organisation's formal reporting procedures for security incidents before you need them. Remember, raising security concerns is never an overreaction - it's a professional responsibility that protects your colleagues and operations.

Building effective defence against deepfake threats requires integrating security practices into your daily operations. Make verification your automatic response to all instructions, not just suspicious ones. This consistent approach ensures you won't skip verification when under pressure. Keep your knowledge of authorised contacts and communication protocols current, and ensure these resources are easily accessible during your shifts. Stay informed about evolving deepfake threats specific to your transport sector through security briefings and industry updates. Practice your recognition and verification skills regularly so they become second nature when you need them most. Finally, help create a workplace culture where questioning instructions is seen as professional diligence, not mistrust. When verification becomes standard practice for everyone, the entire operation becomes more secure and attackers lose their primary advantage of exploiting time pressure and social awkwardness around challenging authority.

Who is this for: Transport operators, logistics coordinators, aviation ground staff, maritime crew members, shipping clerks, freight handlers, truck drivers, warehouse supervisors, air traffic controllers, port workers, dispatch operators
Team or Dept this is for: Transportation, Logistics, Operations, Aviation Operations, Maritime Operations, Shipping, Fleet Management, Supply Chain
